raw_case:
Unusual pattern: Diffuse pulmonary FDG uptake on PET/CT
A 75-year-old nonsmoker and nonalcoholic female, known to have squamous cell carcinoma of the head and neck metastasized to the lungs, was found to have a mass at the right hilar/infra-hilar region and an unusual pattern of increased mild diffuse 18-fluorodeoxyglucose (18-FDG) uptake at the right mid-lower lobe location without any lung parenchymal abnormalities (confirmed by low dose CT scan of PET-CT). It was suspected to be the lymphangitic spread of the neoplasm; however, spectral detector CT (SDCT) imaging performed later not only confirmed the presence of a mass invading the right inferior pulmonary vein but showed a large area of significantly decreased perfusion in the right middle and lower lung lobes, leading to a change in the diagnosis to right pulmonary venous occlusion.

Introduction

An important imaging modality for identifying cancer metastatic sites and staging is the 18-F-fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG) PET/CT. As FDG is an analog of glucose and is injected in the cancer patient undergoing PET/CT, an abnormally increased uptake at various sites of the body indicates metastatic cancerous lesions. The pathophysiological mechanism behind this is that the cancerous cells induce the expression of a large number of glucose transporters as well as hexokinase and phosphofructokinase proteins, leading to metabolic trapping and accumulation of FDG inside the cells once phosphorylated. However, it is important to keep in mind that FDG is not cancer-specific as various normal tissues having metabolically active cells like brown fat, kidneys, spleen, liver, intestine, and urinary bladder, etc. can also show increased FDG radioactivity. Additionally, various nonmalignant pathologies like infections, granulomatous lesions, autoimmune inflammatory lesions, sarcoid reactions, radiation pneumonitis, and reactive hyperplasia also result in increased FDG radioactivity. This is because infections, autoimmune conditions, and radiation pneumonitis result in increased neutrophils in the inflamed area while granulomatous and sarcoid lesions result in an increased number of macrophages, both of which are metabolically very active to cause an increased FDG uptake. Similarly, congestive pathologies like congestive venous thromboembolism can also cause FDG trapping inside the congested organ.Thus, it is extremely important to keep in mind the false positive FDG PET/CT cases and exclude noncancerous etiologies while interpreting PET/CT images of a cancer patient. In this report, we highlight an interesting case of a nonsmoker, nonalcoholic female with known squamous cell carcinoma of head and neck metastasis to the lungs and a positive paternal history of lung cancer, whose PET/CT was falsely positive for lymphangitic spread of cancer due to an increased FDG radioactivity seen in the right mid-lower lobe lung tissue; however, a follow-up spectral detector computed tomography (SDCT) proved it to be a localized accumulation of FDG in the tissue due to blockage of the venous drainage.

Case report

This is a case of a 75-year-old hypertensive female with a past medical history of deep venous thrombosis (DVT) and p16-negative invasive moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the right lateral side of the tongue that was treated with resection and adjuvant chemo-radiotherapy. Following the primary cancer treatment, carboplatin therapy was initiated. However, a PET scan done post-treatment of the primary malignancy found multiple small hypermetabolic metastatic lung lesions in the apex (not shown in Fig. 1) and a large right hilar lymph node (Fig. 1). Her cancer was found to be at Stage III.Fine needle aspiration of the right hilar lymph node proved the diagnosis of metastatic SCC. The metastatic lesions were treated with palliative radiotherapy and nivolumab chemotherapy. During her hospital stay, the patient got atrial fibrillation for which she was started on apixaban anticoagulation. Later, she reported to her attending oncologist having a dry cough that had been increasing for a week. Her cough was not associated with any sputum or blood and she denied having any fever, chills, headache, nausea/vomiting, or abdominal pain. However, she confirmed that she frequently needed to catch up her breath while walking. In the setting of her cancer status and prior history of DVT, a suspicion of chronic pulmonary embolism was made for which a spectral detector dual-energy CT pulmonary angiography (DE-CTPA) was ordered (Fig. 2).

Discussion

One of the most common sites of head and neck malignancy is squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the tongue. The typical sites of tongue malignancy are the tongue tip and lateral sides where etiologies like smoking, chronic alcoholism, and human papillomavirus infection can induce dysplastic changes for the development of cancer [1]. Classical histopathologic features of SCC are keratin pearls and nests of abnormal squamous cells within the fibrous stroma. An important aspect of our case was that the patient had never smoked and denied any alcohol drinking or illicit drug use. However, there was a family history of lung cancer in her father who smoked, and stomach cancer in her maternal grandfather, suggesting a possible genetic etiology. Literature suggests that mutations in the tumor suppressor genes such as TP53, CKDN2A, and LRP1B play an important role in the development of oral SCC while activating mutations in the proto-oncogenes like MET result in the progression and metastasis [2]. Another important aspect to note here is that head and neck squamous cell cancers are highly notorious for having the potential to metastasize, especially in the lungs. This is because TGF-β released by the tumor cells expedites the epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), allowing the cancerous cells to detach from the primary tumor focus and spread to multiple sites of the body using the lymphatic system. Metastasis of head and neck SCC to different body sites has an incidence of about 10% at the initial clinical presentation [3]. Also, secondary metastatic lung lesions are found in approximately 30%-55% of patients after a diagnosis of a primary malignancy [4,5]. In our case, even though the patient had already been provided surgical resection and chemoradiotherapy treatment, the patient experienced metastasis to the lungs.Evaluation of the extent of metastasis and staging of the cancer is crucial for surgical and/or chemo-radiotherapeutic management of the cancer. For this purpose, FDG PET/CT has been investigated as a modality of diagnosing metastatic lesions as it offers high-resolution imaging that allows both anatomic and functional assessment of soft tissues, allowing a comprehensive evaluation with valuable clinical information that supersedes either of the modalities used individually [[6], [7], [8], [9], [10]]. However, patient management can be pivotally changed based on the images provided and recent evidence suggests that up to 27% of cancer patients experience a change in management based on diagnostic imaging findings [11]. In our case, SDCT images helped in making distinction between the suspected lymphangitic spread of neoplasm and pulmonary venous congestion. If the SDCT had not made this distinction, the patient might have been restaged to a more severe cancer stage and her oncologists might have opted for more rigorous treatment options, like pulmonary metastasectomy. It also helped in finding that the perfusion defect was larger than the area of PET uptake, suggesting a worsening of pulmonary vein occlusion by the growing mass. Interestingly, this transitioned the oncologists’ focus to perform radiation therapy toward the mass to relieve the patient's symptoms as well as salvage the lung from becoming completely infarcted. Had SDCT not been performed, the lung would have become infarcted which could have changed the management plan completely.Literature suggests that SDCT also has applications in identifying various other cancers and differentiating them from similarly-presenting benign diseases [12]. Another study found iodine quantitative parameters of SDCT to be highly diagnostic in differentiating metastatic and nonmetastatic lymph nodes [13], indicating the potential of SDCT applications in oncological medicine.

Conclusion

Our case highlights an unusual pattern of FDG uptake in the lung secondary to pulmonary venous occlusion without an underlying neoplastic process and the clinical importance of FDG PET/CT as well as SDCT in patient management. This also underscores the importance of earlier use of SDCT in cases where pulmonary venous occlusion is suspected. SDCT can also be utilized in other pulmonary vascular abnormalities like pulmonary arterial hypertension or pulmonary venous thrombosis where the iodine mapping can help in identifying the areas with significantly decreased perfusion.

Patient consent

The corresponding author confirms that every attempt was made to reach out to the patient's daughter (as the patient is no longer alive) but she couldn't be contacted.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 75-year-old female, nonsmoker, nonalcoholic.
- **Chief Complaint:** Dry cough without sputum or blood.
- **History of Present Illness:** Progressively worsening dry cough over one week, associated with breathlessness on exertion. No fever, chills, headache, nausea/vomiting, or abdominal pain.
- **Past Medical History:** Hypertension, deep venous thrombosis (DVT), p16-negative squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the tongue previously treated with surgery, chemo-radiotherapy, and carboplatin therapy; metastatic SCC to the lungs; atrial fibrillation on apixaban; palliative radiotherapy and nivolumab.
- **Personal History:** Denies smoking, alcohol use, or drug use.
- **Family History:** Paternal history of lung cancer (smoker), maternal history of stomach cancer.
- **Physical Examination:** No significant findings reported.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - **PET/CT:** Mass at right hilar/infra-hilar region; mild diffuse FDG uptake in the right mid-lower lobe; multiple hypermetabolic metastatic lesions in the lungs.
  - **CT Imaging:** Showed no lung parenchymal abnormalities.
  - **Spectral Detector CT (SDCT):** Confirmed mass invading the right inferior pulmonary vein with significantly decreased perfusion in the right middle and lower lobes, consistent with right pulmonary venous occlusion.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Suspected lymphangitic spread of neoplasm:**
   - Initial consideration based on PET/CT findings of diffuse FDG uptake. However, low-dose CT showed no lung parenchymal abnormalities, raising doubts about this assumption.
   - Spectral Detector CT (SDCT) findings ruled out lymphangitic spread by demonstrating a mass invading the right inferior pulmonary vein and decreased perfusion specific to pulmonary venous occlusion.

2. **Chronic pulmonary embolism:**
   - Considered due to the history of DVT and symptoms of breathlessness, but SDCT did not show evidence of thromboembolism.

3. **Pulmonary venous occlusion:**
   - Confirmed by SDCT showing a mass invading the vein with decreased lung perfusion. PET/CT FDG uptake was misleading, as it likely represented localized FDG trapping due to congestion secondary to venous obstruction.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Right pulmonary venous occlusion caused by a mass invading the right inferior pulmonary vein, secondary to metastatic squamous cell carcinoma. Pulmonary venous congestion led to a false-positive PET/CT interpretation of lymphangitic spread.

checked_rare_disease: Squamous cell carcinoma of the oral cavity

results.content:
> Reasoning
**Analyzing patient history**
I’m gathering insights from the patient's history and current condition to reach a precise diagnosis. This leads me to consider the next steps: outlining potential diagnoses or suggesting diagnostic processes.
**Taking a closer look**
I'm about to carefully review the patient's case summary to provide an accurate diagnosis, considering all symptoms and history for a thorough analysis.
**Taking a closer look**
I’m systematically examining the patient's case summary, considering the person's medical history, symptoms, and other health details to ensure a precise diagnosis or plan of action.
**Analyzing patient details**
I’m carefully reviewing the patient's medical history, symptoms, and previous diagnoses to identify potential causes for the patient's current condition and ensure a comprehensive approach to diagnosis.
**Assessing patient details**
I’m examining the patient's medical history, current symptoms, and previous diagnoses to pinpoint potential causes and form a comprehensive diagnosis strategy.
**Evaluating patient findings**
Reviewing the case summary to identify clues about the patient's condition and pinpointing the root cause of his symptoms. This helps in forming an accurate diagnosis or treatment plan.
**Taking a closer look**
Carefully reviewing the patient's medical history, current symptoms, and previous diagnoses to identify potential causes and develop an effective diagnosis or treatment plan.
**Analyzing medical clues**
I’m piecing together the patient's medical history, symptoms, and previous diagnoses to identify potential causes and formulate a comprehensive diagnosis or treatment plan.
Reasoned for 9 seconds

### Reasoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with a progressively worsening dry cough over the past week, associated with exertional breathlessness. This suggests a pulmonary or cardiovascular cause, as these symptoms can arise from conditions affecting lung function or ventilation. The absence of fever, chills, headache, nausea/vomiting, or abdominal pain reduces the likelihood of infections or gastrointestinal issues.

<step 2> The patient’s medical history is significant for metastatic squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) of the tongue with metastases to the lungs. This is critical because the history of metastatic cancer raises concern for pulmonary complications related to the malignancy, such as progression of the lung metastases or new tumor formations. The presence of a mass at the right hilar/infra-hilar region and multiple hypermetabolic lesions in the lungs on the PET/CT further supports this suspicion, suggesting an active metastatic process.

<step 3> The CT scan findings show no lung parenchymal abnormalities, which may suggest that the symptoms are not primarily caused by conditions like pneumonia or primary lung disease. However, the Spectral Detector CT (SDCT) confirmed a mass invading the right inferior pulmonary vein, with significantly decreased perfusion in the right middle and lower lobes. This is highly suggestive of pulmonary venous occlusion, likely due to the mass, which could be causing the patient's breathlessness and dry cough due to impaired pulmonary circulation and ventilation.

<step 4> The combination of known metastatic SCC, the mass at the right hilar/infra-hilar region, and the findings of pulmonary venous occlusion is concerning for complications arising from the patient's cancer. The worsening dry cough, breathlessness on exertion, and imaging findings of venous occlusion point toward a diagnosis of lung metastasis with associated obstructive pulmonary venous pathology.

### Answer:
Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma with pulmonary venous occlusion.
